data.title: Algorae Pharmaceuticals strengthens leadership team with appointment of new chief commercial officer
data.description: Algorae Pharmaceuticals has strengthened its leadership team with the appointment of seasoned pharmaceutical executive Vishal Shah to the role of chief commercial officer. Mr Shah has over 20 years’ experience in the pharmaceuticals, biotechnology and healthcare industries. He will spearhead Algorae’s commercial strategy to accelerate growth and expansion into complementary pharmaceutical market segments. Previous roles Mr Shah was previously employed by Australasian pharmaceutical wholesaler and distributor HPS Pharmacies, where he was instrumental in accelerating the business’s expansion into new markets, forging high-value partnerships and responsible for shaping the company’s procurement strategy and driving revenue growth. Mr Shah has also held senior leadership roles at medical technology giant Baxter Healthcare, including associate director of pharmaceuticals for Asia-Pacific and head of pharmaceuticals for Australia–New Zealand. He helped lead commercial strategy for the $27 billion firm, executing major product launches, managing growth initiatives to enhance revenue and earnings, establishing strategic alliances and optimising commercial operations across international markets. Pivotal time Mr Shah – who has a pharmacy degree, a master’s in applied science (biotechnology) and an MBA in business administration from Western Sydney University – said he was excited to join Algorae at a pivotal time in the company’s growth. “Algorae’s artificial-intelligence-driven pharmaceutical strategy offers a competitive edge in transforming drug development and market penetration and I look forward to working with the team to drive commercial success and expand our impact,” he said. Deeply engaged in shaping the future of healthcare, Mr Shah is a member of the advisory committee for Access Capital Venture in Australia. He also provides strategic counsel to emerging health-tech startups, helping them navigate the complexities of commercialisation and market entry. Right combination Algorae executive chair David Hainsworth said Mr Shah possessed the right combination of scientific expertise and commercial acumen for his new role with the company. “Vishal’s extensive experience in scaling up pharmaceutical businesses, forging strategic partnerships and driving revenue growth will be instrumental in propelling Algorae’s artificial intelligence-driven innovation into market success,” he said.

data.title: Sensex falls 400 pts, Nifty at 23,200: Trump’s reciprocal tariffs, other factors rattle markets
data.description:
Benchmark indices Sensex and Nifty slipped in early trade on Thursday, tracking weak global cues after US President Donald Trump imposed reciprocal tariffs on about 60 countries, including India. The move dented sentiment in domestic equities, with IT stocks leading the losses.

At 10 AM, the BSE Sensex was down 348.86 points or 0.46 percent at 76,268.58, while the broader NSE Nifty fell 103.70 points or 0.44 percent to 23,228.65.

Among the major laggards were Tata Consultancy Services, Infosys, Tech Mahindra, HCL Technologies, Tata Motors, Adani Ports, Bharti Airtel, Reliance Industries, Maruti Suzuki, Zomato, and Kotak Mahindra Bank.

Key Factors Behind Market Decline:

1) Trump’s Reciprocal Tariffs: The US announced a 26 percent reciprocal tariff on India, citing high import duties imposed by New Delhi on American goods. While pharma imports from India are exempt, other exports could face pressure.

"The tariffs are significantly higher than anticipated and will disrupt global trade, impacting growth," Ankita Pathak, Macro Strategist and Global Equities Fund Advisor at Ionic Asset told Reuters. "This is likely to affect India’s domestic growth, corporate earnings, and valuations."

2) Rupee Under Pressure: The Indian rupee fell 26 paise to 85.78 against the US dollar in early trade following Trump’s tariff decision. Forex traders noted that the move sparked risk aversion, prompting investors to seek safer assets.

3) Weak Global Cues: Asian markets mirrored the sell-off, with Japan’s Nikkei plunging over 3 percent, Hong Kong’s Hang Seng dropping 2 percent and South Korea’s KOSPI declining by 1 percent. China’s Shanghai Composite fell 0.39 percent. US Dow Jones Futures were also down 2 percent, signaling further pressure on global equities.

Market Outlook

Technical analysts remain cautious despite the volatility. Anand James, Chief Market Strategist at Geojit Investments, said, "While Nifty formed an inside bar candlestick pattern yesterday, broader market risk appetite appears to be improving. If Nifty manages to stay above 23,270 after early volatility, a move towards 23,450 is possible. However, a slide below 23,050-22,960 could indicate further downside. Strong support is expected around 22,730-22,650."Disclaimer: The views and investment tips expressed by experts on Moneycontrol are their own and not those of the website or its management. Moneycontrol advises users to check with certified experts before taking any investment decisions.

data.title: Taiwan says US tariffs unreasonable, partly blames Trump policies for trade surplus
data.description:
Key points:
  • Taiwan says to discuss 'unreasonable' tariffs with US
  • Taiwan subject to new 32% tariff by Trump
  • Semiconductors are excluded from tariffs
  • Tech powerhouse Taiwan runs large trade surplus with US

Taiwan's government said on Thursday that U.S. tariffs levied on the island were unreasonable and it would discuss them with Washington, partly blaming U.S. tech curbs on China in President Donald Trump's first term for driving the trade imbalance.

Trump on Wednesday announced across the board import tariffs, with much higher duties for dozens of trading partners, including Taiwan which runs a large trade surplus with the United States and will have a 32% duty placed on its products.

The U.S. tariffs, however, do not apply to semiconductors, a major Taiwan export.

Taiwan's cabinet said in a statement that it regretted the "unreasonable" U.S. tariffs and it would seek clarification and continue talks with Washington to ensure Taiwan's interests were protected.

How the United States has calculated the tariffs was unclear and did not reflect the complementary trade structure between the two sides, the cabinet said.

Taiwan's exports to and trade surplus with the United States have increased because of U.S. demand for semiconductors, and artificial intelligence-related products, as well as Trump's first-term tariffs and controls on China, it added.

This resulted in "the shift of Taiwan's supply chain back to Taiwan and an increase in U.S. demand for Taiwan's information and communications products, reflecting the huge contribution of Taiwan to the U.S. economy and national security", the cabinet said.

During Trump's first term in office from 2017 to 2021, he placed some Chinese companies on trade blacklists that curbed their access to crucial U.S. hardware and software, benefiting U.S. ally Taiwan as orders shifted to Taiwanese firms.

Taiwanese government officials have repeatedly said trade with the United States has been skewed by an insatiable demand for Taiwanese technology products, such as advanced semiconductors - a sector dominated by the island, home to major chipmaker TSMC .

TSMC last month announced a new $100 billion investment in the United States.

The company declined to comment on the tariffs, saying it was in its quiet period ahead of its first-quarter earnings on April 17.

In a separate statement after the tariff announcement, the American Chamber of Commerce in Taiwan said it continued to stress Taiwan's "indispensable" role in the U.S. economy.

"In a time of growing geopolitical complexity, the U.S.-Taiwan partnership is not only a driver of shared economic prosperity but also central to supply chain security and regional stability," it said in a statement.

Trump's announcement followed the end of the latest round of Chinese war games around Taiwan, which Beijing claims as its own territory despite the objections of Taipei's government.

data.title: HK-listed e-commerce groups fall on Trump's reciprocal tariffs
data.description:
** Hong Kong-listed shares of Chinese e-commerce groups fall after U.S. President Donald Trump announced reciprocal tariffs

** Alibaba's Hong Kong stock falls 6% to HK$122.2, its lowest level since February 20, and JD.com Hong Kong-listed shares also drop 6% to HK$152.2 to their lowest since February 25

** Trump signed an executive order on Wednesday that closes a trade loophole used to ship low-value packages, those valued at $800 or less, duty-free from China known as "de minimis". The order covers goods from China and Hong Kong and will take effect on May 2

** Hong Kong-listed shares of express delivery group SF Holdings fall 3.6% to HK$37, the biggest intraday pct drop since debut on November 27

** Hong Kong-listed stock of household appliances maker Haier Smart Home drops 8% to HK$22.9, its lowest since September 11

** Hisense Home Appliances' Hong Kong shares plunge 8.8% to HK$23.25, lowest since December 9

** Midea Group falls 5.9% to HK$72.05, its lowest since March 14

** Hang Seng Tech Index declines 2.2%, Hang Seng Index falls 1.6%

data.title: Mongolian Mining Corp. and Energy Resources Files for Singapore Listing of $350 Million Bonds
data.description:
Mongolian Mining Corp. and Energy Resources filed for the listing of $350 million worth of 8.44% fixed rate guaranteed senior bonds due 2030, on the Singapore bourse, according to a filing with the Singapore Exchange on Thursday.

The bonds will be listed and quoted in the Bonds Market on April 4, the filing added.

data.title: QBE Insurance Files for Singapore Listing of $500 Million Bonds
data.description:
QBE Insurance filed for the listing of $500 million worth of fixed-rate resetting subordinated bonds due 2035 on the Singapore bourse, according to a filing with the Singapore Exchange on Thursday.

The bonds will be listed and quoted in the Bonds Market on April 4, the filing stated.

data.title: CATL, Sinopec Enter Partnership to Build Chinese Battery Swap Network
data.description:
Contemporary Amperex Technology , or CATL, and China Petroleum and Chemical , or Sinopec, signed a strategic cooperation agreement to accelerate China's battery-swapping ecosystem.

The partnership aims to establish at least 500 battery swap stations in 2025, with a long-term goal of reaching 10,000 stations nationwide, according to a Wednesday news release.

The collaboration will leverage Sinopec's energy infrastructure and CATL's battery technology, including Choco-Swap and QiJi battery solutions, to enhance efficiency for passenger vehicles and heavy-duty trucks.

The partnership will integrate energy supply with smart vehicle technology to support China's "dual-carbon" goals and the development of a cost-effective energy replenishment network, according to the news release.

data.title: US slaps 26% tariff on India, lower than Asian peers
data.description:
Key points:
  • India's non-tariff barriers hurt US exports, White House says
  • US move will add pressure on India to speed up trade talks
  • US move could push India towards other trade blocs, expert says
  • Indian markets largely unperturbed, recover after initial losses

The U.S. slapped a 26% tariff on imports from India in a setback to the South Asian nation's expectation of getting relief from President Donald Trump's global trade policy that has unnerved world markets for weeks.

A baseline 10% baseline tariff starts on Saturday before the remaining, higher reciprocal tariff takes effect from April 9.

"They (India) are charging us 52% and we charge almost nothing for years and years and decades," Trump said at the White House while announcing the reciprocal tax.

Trump has slapped higher duties on other countries, including 34% on China on top of a 20% tax announced earlier, and 46% on Vietnam.

The relatively lower tariffs imposed on India calmed equity markets.

India's benchmark share indexes, the Nifty 50 and the BSE Sensex , were down 0.3% each at open, lower than the 1.5% to 3% losses seen across other Asian markets. The rupee fell as much as 0.3% to 85.75 against the dollar in early trade but recovered to 85.65.

India gains a natural competitive advantage in several key sectors due to the relatively lower tariffs imposed, research firm Global Trade Research Institute said in a note.

Nearly $14 billion worth of electronics products and over $9 billion worth of gems and jewellery are among the top sectors to be hit by the U.S. tariffs.

The Trump administration, however, exempted pharmaceutical exports from the tariff bringing cheer to India's pharma industry. The U.S. accounted for nearly a third of India's pharmaceutical exports - mainly cheaper versions of popular drugs - with sales of about $9 billion last fiscal year.

Shares of Indian drugmakers rose nearly 5% in early trade on Thursday in contrast to the drop in the broader stock market.

India's export competitiveness would be less impacted than key rivals due to its position in the middle of the tariff rates, said the country's industry bodies, Assocham and the Federation of Indian Export Organisations.

The duty of 26% was based on tariff and non-tariff barriers including currency manipulation, the Trump administration said.

India imposed "uniquely burdensome" non-tariff barriers, the removal of which will increase U.S. exports by at least $5.3 billion annually, the White House said.

The tariffs would remain in effect until Trump determined that the "threat posed by the trade deficit and underlying non-reciprocal treatment is satisfied, resolved, or mitigated," the statement added.

The U.S. has a trade deficit of $46 billion with India.

AMONG TRUMP'S FRIENDS

The reciprocal tariff will add pressure on Indian Prime Minister Narendra Modi, who counts himself among Trump's friends, to find ways to get India off the hook.

Last week Reuters reported that New Delhi is open to cutting tariffs on U.S. imports worth $23 billion to mitigate the impact on its exports in sectors like gems and jewellery, pharmaceuticals and auto parts.

Modi's administration has taken a number of steps to win over Trump by lowering tariffs on high-end bikes, bourbon and dropping a tax on digital services that affected U.S. tech giants.

Before the reciprocal announcement, the U.S. tariff rates were among the lowest, with simple average tariffs at 3.3%, compared with India's 17%, the White House said.

Ajay Sahai, director general at the Federation of India Export Organisations, said the reciprocal tariff on India was lower than key competitors like Vietnam and Bangladesh, which could help Indian apparel and footwear sectors.

data.title: Japan, South Korea Likely to Try to Negotiate Tariffs, ING SaysMarket Talk
data.description: Both Japan and South Korea will look to reduce Trump's reciprocal tariffs rather than seek to hit back at the respective 24% and 25% levies, ING's Min Joo Kang says. The marginal impact of the new tariffs is likely to be smaller than the separate 25% duty on autos, which is the largest category of exports to the U.S. for both countries, the economist says. Semiconductors and pharma--both major export items from Japan and Korea--have been spared, she notes, expecting the negative impact to be more concentrated in heavy industry and machinery. "Neither country is likely to retaliate," she adds, instead seeing them making concessions by importing more U.S. products. (fabiana.negrinochoa@wsj.com)

data.title: Galileo Mining Says Drilling Extends Platinum, Palladium Mineralization at Western Australia Project; Shares Fall Past 8%
data.description:
Galileo Mining said assay results from air core drilling at its Norseman project in Western Australia extended the known palladium and platinum mineralization at the Mission Sill prospect, according to a Thursday Australian bourse filing.

About 4,500 meters of air core drilling in 159 drill holes were conducted in the March campaign.

"Significant" intercepts included 4 meters at 1.2 grams per tonne (g/t). grade of palladium-platinum-gold from 28 meters and 12 meters at 0.4 g/t grade of palladium-platinum-gold from the surface.

Galileo Mining's shares fell over 8% in recent trading on Thursday.

data.title: Dabur falls after flagging weak fourth-quarter India business growth
data.description:
** Shares of Dabur India fall 7.2% to 460.15 rupees, heads for fourth straight session of losses

** Consumer goods major flagged a drop in India FMCG business growth due to "delayed and truncated winters and a slowdown in urban markets"

** Consolidated revenue expected to be flattish during the quarter

** Expects Q4 operating profit margin to contract by around 150-175 basis points y/y

** Emkay Global cuts PT for DABU to 450 rupees - 9.3% below previous close - from 500 rupees; keeps "reduce" rating

data.title: ACME Solar Secures INR24.9 Billion Project Financing; Shares Jump 5%
data.description:
Indian renewable energy company ACME Solar Holdings has secured 24.9 billion Indian rupees in term project finance facility from the State Bank of India and REC , according to a Thursday filing to the Indian stock exchanges.

The funding will be used to refinance the company's existing debt facilities and reduce the cost for financing its 490 MW renewable energy projects in Andhra Pradesh, Rajasthan, and Punjab.

The sum is also expected to help improve the ACME Solar's credit profile and obtain higher credit ratings for Andhra Pradesh and Punjab entities, ACME said.

Shares of the company were up 5% in recent trade.

data.title: Bioxyne Signs AU$7 Million Manufacturing, Supply Agreement
data.description:
Bioxyne signed an exclusive AU$7 million manufacturing and supply agreement with its existing customer, NectarTek Australia, according to a Thursday Australian bourse filing.

Its unit Breathe Life Sciences was named as the exclusive manufacturer of all NectarTek products for distribution in Australia, the UK, and Germany.

The agreement includes volume commitments which are expected to generate at least AU$7 million in revenue for Bioxyne in the fiscal year 2026.

The contract includes minimum order volumes of AU$3.6 million of pastilles, AU$2.5 million of packed flower products, AU$500,000 of vapes, and AU$500,000 of oral mucosal oils and other products.

data.title: UK Stocks-Factors to watch on April 3
data.description:
Britain's FTSE 100 CURRENCYCOM:UK100 index is seen opening lower on Thursday, with futures down 1.48%.

* TARIFFS: Britain's Business Secretary Jonathan Reynolds vowed to "remain calm" and insisted he still wants a trade deal with the United States after President Donald Trump decided to impose import tariffs of 10% on the United Kingdom.

* OIL: Oil prices fell by as much as 3% after U.S. President Donald Trump announced sweeping new tariffs, sparking fears of a global trade war that will curtail economic growth and limit fuel demand.

* GOLD: Investors flocked to safe-haven assets and pushed gold to a record high on more aggressive-than-expected import tariffs kicking into higher gear an heated global trade war.

* METALS: Copper prices in London dropped as sweeping U.S. reciprocal tariffs sparked concerns about global demand for metals.

* EX-DIVS: Rentokil Initial ,Smiths Group , Barratt Redrow , Mondi , IMI , Direct Line Insurance Group , Ithaca Energy , Breedon Group , Domino's Pizza Group , Pollen Street and Chesnara will trade without entitlement to their latest dividend pay-out on Thursday.

* The UK blue-chip index CURRENCYCOM:UK100 closed down 0.3% on Wednesday as investors awaited details of U.S. President Donald Trump's tariff plans in the latest escalation of a global trade war that has heightened worries about global growth and inflation.

data.title: Benz Mining Hits High-Grade Gold Mineralization at Glenburgh Project in Western Australia; Shares Rise 11%
data.description:
Benz Mining Corp identified high-grade gold mineralization at the Glenburgh project in Western Australia following its maiden drilling program, according to a Thursday filing with the Australian bourse.

Significant intercepts include 11 meters at 19.9 grams per tonne grade of gold from a depth of 274 meters, five meters at 10.2 grams per tonne grade of gold from 222 meters, and seven meters at 3.5 grams per tonne grade of gold from 233 meters, the filing said.

Assays from the remaining holes are expected by the end of the June quarter.

Benz Mining's shares rose 11% in recent trade.

data.title: Nextensa to Buy Office Buildings, Lease Space in Belgium Under Deal with Proximus
data.description:
Nextensa agreed to buy office buildings from and rent office spaces to Belgian technology and telecommunications company Proximus.

Under the deal, the Belgian real estate company is purchasing Proximus' current headquarters at North Station in Belgium for 62.5 million euros, according to a Wednesday release. The agreement includes a profit-sharing mechanism that will allow Proximus to receive additional proceeds in case of a significant improvement in the real estate market.

Nextensa intends to redevelop the buildings into a 115,000-square-meter mixed-use project that will be renamed Bel Towers. The company will take over the existing planning and environmental permits for the buildings from fellow developer Immobel for 18 million euros to proceed with the development.

Meanwhile, Nextensa also agreed to lease to Proximus a combined 44,000 square meters of its Tour & Taxis site in Brussels to serve as the new headquarters of the tech and telecoms company. The lease has a 15-year term, with Proximus to begin relocating in March 2027.

data.title: A tiny Australian outpost gets a steep Trump tariff despite few exports
data.description:
Key points:
  • Reciprocal tariffs of 29% levied on Norfolk Island's exports to US
  • US data shows it has had trade deficits with island for past 3 years
  • Island's main industry is tourism, some of its business owners say

For Richard Cottle, owner of a concrete-mixing business on Norfolk Island, there was only one explanation when U.S. President Donald Trump unveiled a hefty 29% tariff on the tiny territory about 600 miles off eastern Australia: "it was just a mistake".

Though the rugged volcanic island in the southern Pacific does ship a modest amount of Kentia palm seeds abroad, typically worth less than $1 million a year, mostly to Europe, news of the unusually steep tariff passed through its 2,188 residents on Thursday with a mixture of amusement and confusion.

"Norfolk Island is a little dot in the world," said Cottle by phone. "We don't export anything."

Norfolk Island was among dozens of tiny territories which appeared on the same list as China and the European Union as recipients of Trump's highly anticipated tariff regime, even though they do not have a real manufacturing or export industry.

Some, like the Heard and McDonald Islands in the Antarctic, which like Norfolk Island is overseen by Australia, did not even have human inhabitants. No matter - as of Thursday, they faced a 10% tariff for exports to the U.S.

Australian Prime Minister Anthony Albanese, on the campaign trail ahead of an election in a month, told the media his country did better than most with a tariff of 10% - half of what the EU was hit with and one-third of what China got - but he had no explanation for Norfolk Island.

"Last time I looked, Norfolk Island was a part of Australia," he told the Australian Broadcasting Corp. The separate, higher tariff "was somewhat unexpected and a bit strange", he added.

According to U.S. government data, the U.S. has recorded trade deficits with Norfolk Island for the past three years. The island exported $300,000 worth of goods to the U.S. in 2022, $700,000 in 2023 and $200,000 in 2024. Its imports from the U.S. stayed at $100,000 in those years.

Norfolk Island's imports from the U.S. peaked at $11.7 million in 2020, when no exports were recorded. The data did not specify what goods were traded.

Other Norfolk Island business owners who spoke with Reuters could think of no manufacturing industry on the island, and added that its main industry by far was tourism. One pest control business owner, who asked not to be named, said that although they did not export to the U.S., they imported some rodent bait from the U.S. via Australia.

"Products from Norfolk Island are going to have a 29% tariff? Well, there is no product, so it's not going to have an effect," said Gye Duncan, who owns a tax consultancy on the island.

"They probably don't even know where Norfolk Island is in the world. It's just probably an anomaly."

data.title: Instabank Partners Smartmiete on Digital Rental Deposit Account Services in Germany
data.description:
Instabank partnered with Smartmiete to launch digital rental deposit account services in Germany.

The launch is part of the Norwegian lender's broader Northern European growth strategy, according to a Wednesday release.

data.title: Sensex Falls After Trump Announces 26% Reciprocal Tariff
data.description:
India's shares dropped 327 points, or 0.4%, to 76,300 in morning trade on Thursday, halting gains from the previous session, following U.S. President Trump's imposition of a 26% reciprocal tariff on India.

Additionally, Trump announced a 25% tariff on auto component imports to the country.

The BSE Sensex tracked a slump in U.S. futures, mainly weighed down by the tech, metals, and auto sectors.

Trump's tariffs raised concerns about a global trade war after China announced plans for "resolute" countermeasures against the Trump administration’s tariffs on Chinese goods, aiming to protect its interests.

However, fresh data from India's top trading partner, China, limited the fall after a private survey showed that the private sector in the second-largest economy grew the most in four months.

The Nifty 50 dropped 0.4% to below 23,300, with notable losses from Tata Consultancy Services (-2.4%), Infosys (-2.4%), HCL Tech (-2.3%), Tech Mahindra (-2.1%), and Tata Motors (-1.6%).

data.title: Average American Family Faces Big Jump in Costs Due to TariffsMarket Talk
data.description: The average American family may pay up to an estimated $4,200 more per year because of Trump's tariffs, assuming an average 20% tariff rate on imports, says Stephen Dover, chief market strategist and head of Franklin Templeton Institute. Tariffs, will likely slow household and business spending, and increase the risk of U.S. growth and earnings disappointments ahead, Dover says. The U.S. services trade especially banks, consulting, and technology companies could be vulnerable to restrictions from other countries, while deliberate shunning of U.S. products and companies might also emerge, he adds. (james.glynn@wsj.com; @JamesGlynnWSJ)

data.title: Note Appointed Exclusive Production Partner for Waystream
data.description:
Note said Wednesday that it was appointed as the exclusive production partner for Swedish telecom company Waystream's products.

The appointment marks an increased collaboration between the electronics manufacturing services provider and Waystream.

data.title: Key to Outlook for Risk Assets: Tariff Retaliation or NegotiationMarket Talk
data.description:
0446 GMT - Whether the likes of the European Union and China retaliate or negotiate with President Trump on tariffs "will be important for risk assets," says Wilsons Advisory head of investment strategy David Cassidy. "A big retaliation push will be incrementally negative for risk assets, while a tilt to negotiation would be positively received by investors," Cassidy says. China has already come out swinging against the tariffs, saying it will take resolute measures in response. As it stands, the sweeping tariffs plan looks like it will be "incrementally negative for risk assets albeit, at least there is some certainty now in terms of the quantum of the tariff impost," Cassidy says. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

0444 GMT - The average American family may pay up to an estimated $4,200 more per year because of Trump's tariffs, assuming an average 20% tariff rate on imports, says Stephen Dover, chief market strategist and head of Franklin Templeton Institute. Tariffs, will likely slow household and business spending, and increase the risk of U.S. growth and earnings disappointments ahead, Dover says. The U.S. services trade especially banks, consulting, and technology companies could be vulnerable to restrictions from other countries, while deliberate shunning of U.S. products and companies might also emerge, he adds. (james.glynn@wsj.com; @JamesGlynnWSJ)

0437 GMT - The arrival of "Liberation Day" and a dizzying array of U.S. tariff hikes looks like a headache for economic forecasters. "You can throw most forecasts out the door, if this tariff rate stays on for an extended period of time," says Olu Sonola, Fitch Ratings' head of U.S. economic research. The U.S. tariff rate on all imports has sky-rocketed to around 22% from 2.5% last year, he writes. Tariffs haven't reached that level since about 1910, Sonola notes. That's a game changer, he says, not only for the U.S. but for the global economy, putting many countries at potential risk of recession. (fabiana.negrinochoa@wsj.com)

0410 GMT - China may announce additional fiscal stimulus to counteract perceived macroeconomic weakness, providing some relief to the market and consumers, Morningstar Asia equity market strategist Kai Wang writes in a note. In the short term, Chinese markets will react to headline risks, which may not necessarily reflect underlying fundamentals, Wang says. Many Chinese industries remain unaffected, as over 95% of their revenues come from within China, except for sectors such as manufacturing, car batteries and appliances, he says. Despite the absense of direct U.S. exposure for some companies, the key question is whether tariffs will lead to lower export levels to the U.S., Wang says. This could have a ripple effect on overall consumption in China. Morningstar maintains its view that investors should focus on companies poised to benefit directly from any stimulus measures from Beijing.(jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

0343 GMT - China's revised consumer loan rules will provide modest support to domestic consumption and economic activity, says Fitch Ratings. The new rules, which extend the maximum consumer loan term from five to seven years, and raise the limits on individual consumer loan sizes, are likely to increase the availability of credit for some borrowers and expand the overall consumer finance market, the credit rating agency says. However, lenders could face additional risks if the reform leads to looser underwriting standards or an aggressive increase in lending to less creditworthy borrowers, it adds. (tracy.qu@wsj.com)

0332 GMT - Near-term risks from U.S. President Trump's tariff announcement overnight include a global trade war, Grasshopper Asset Management's Daniel Tan says in an email. Such a global trade war "could destabilize global supply chains and hurt U.S. exporters," the portfolio manager says. "In a risk-off scenario, the Japanese yen and U.S. rates should continue to do well," Tan says. The asset manager has been wary in increasing its exposure to emerging markets in the run-up to Wednesday's tariff announcement, Tan says, adding the asset manager's portfolio duration was extended in view of a risk-off scenario. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0320 GMT - Earlier this year, many investors were expecting President Trump's tough trade demands would soften over time, says Damien Hennessy, Zenith Investment Partners head of asset allocation. "However, this somewhat comfortable working assumption has been challenged by the escalation in announced tariffs," he says. The latest tariff plan, "together with the collateral damage we've already seen on consumer and business confidence, and the disappearance of the so-called 'Trump put', has sent markets into a tailspin." Melbourne-based Hennessy says Zenith has raised its estimated probability of U.S. recession to 35%, from 20%, "a level that cannot be ignored." (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

0315 GMT - U.S. reciprocal tariffs unveiled Wednesday may persist as a source of economic headwinds, Mizuho Securities (Singapore)'s Vishnu Varathan says in an email. These tariffs could also be "inadvertently compounded, exacerbating vulnerabilities to adverse demand shocks," says the head of Macro Research for Asia ex-Japan. "Asia is particularly hard-hit, with Cambodia, Vietnam, Thailand and Indonesia featuring," Varathan says. Also, Taiwan, South Korea, Japan, India, and the EU haven't been spared, while the U.K., Australia and Singapore are least impacted, he says. Hence, pressure on Asia ex-Japan currencies could continue, Varathan adds. USD/THB rises 0.2% to 34.35; USD/PHP edges 0.1% higher to 57.22; USD/MYR gains 0.4% to 4.4720. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0312 GMT - Trump's tariffs are arguably much higher than anticipated by the market and will affect Asian exports, says Chris Kushlis, chief emerging markets macro strategist at T. Rowe Price, in an email. Tariff increases in the range of 20%-35% will pose a meaningful headwind to growth this year, especially for more open, trade-oriented economies, he says. A relatively high proportion of many Asian economies' export value ends up in the U.S., so the broad application of tariffs globally will hinder effects to redirect trade, he adds. Risk assets will likely remain under pressure and volatile as companies assess the impact of tariffs on their business plans and the potential time frame for negotiations to cut tariffs, he says. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

0311 GMT - Trump's higher-than-expected tariffs on Chinese products may prompt more forceful retaliation from Beijing and drag on China's growth and inflation this year, says Lynn Song, economist at ING. While Song maintains his forecast that U.S. tariffs likely pose a drag between 0.4-0.8 ppt on China's growth in 2025, and says in a note that this week's developments likely means the impact will be toward the higher end of that band. The potential hit from tariffs will likely prompt more monetary easing from Beijing, says Song, who says rate and RRR cuts could be delivered together for better effect and the PBOC is unlikely to intentionally devalue the yuan to offset tariffs. (singaporeeditors@dowjones.com)

0245 GMT - Australian consumers might benefit from President Trump's latest tariffs, should reduced-price surplus European and Asian goods be redirected away from the U.S. and into Australia, says Whitefield managing director Angus Gluskie. But this could be a negative for Australia's big iron ore producers, who ship vast amounts of the steel ingredient to mills in Asia, he says. Still, the Sydney-based investor reckons the greatest impact on Australia from the sweeping U.S. tariffs is likely to be from how it jolts the global economy. "Disruption and uncertainty are difficult for corporations and consumers," says Gluskie. Tariff-driven inflation in the U.S. may also drive up global interest rates, he says. "So concerns around potential global stagflation are likely to remain the larger concern for investment markets." (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

0232 GMT - The sweeping Trump tariffs could pose higher-than-expected risks to China's exports, says Pinpoint Asset Management chief economist Zhiwei Zhang in a note. The high tariffs on Asean countries, such as Vietnam and Cambodia, could weigh on China's exports to these countries, he says. Supply chains in East Asia are vertically integrated as China's exports of intermediate goods are crucial for these countries' exports, he says. "Retaliation from China on the U.S. tariffs is likely coming," he says. Zhang expects Beijing to push up fiscal spending and cut interest rate and reserve requirement ratio to offset the damage to China's exports, he says. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

data.title: Japan stocks see longest foreign sell-off in 3 years as US tariffs weigh
data.description:
Foreigners sold Japanese stocks for the ninth straight week through March 29, the longest selling run in three years, amid concerns that U.S. reciprocal tariffs could disrupt exports.

U.S. President Trump announced a higher-than-expected 24% tariff on Japanese goods on Wednesday, while a previously imposed 25% tariff on auto imports will take effect as planned on April 3 in a major blow to the Japanese auto industry.

Foreigners sold Japanese stocks worth 450.4 billion yen ($3.06 billion) on a net basis for the week, according to data from Japan's Ministry of Finance.

In the nine straight weeks of net selling that began on January 26, foreigners have withdrawn 6.47 trillion yen compared with just 3.89 trillion yen worth of total net withdrawals in the week through March 26, 2022, the last nine-week-long selling streak.

The Nikkei tumbled as much as 4.6% to an eight-month low of 34,102.00 on the day, with analysts warning of further pressure as a stronger yen, driven by safe-haven demand, threatened to weigh on exports.

Foreign investors also ditched Japanese bonds in the week ended March 29, as they shed long-term bonds worth 489.7 billion yen and short-term bills of 155.8 billion yen.

Meanwhile, Japanese investors net bought foreign stocks worth 583.2 billion yen, marking the sixth weekly net purchase in seven weeks.

However, they sold foreign debt, for the second successive week, worth about 214.9 billion yen.

($1 = 147.3600 yen)

data.title: Rio Tinto Invests in Iron Ore Rail Car Construction in Australia
data.description:
Rio Tinto said Thursday its first Western Australian-built iron ore rail car was constructed to boost local manufacturing.

The mining giant is investing AU$150 million to buy 100 locally built rail cars and continue bearing refurbishment for its Pilbara operations. Rio Tinto partnered with Engenco's Gemco Rail and CRRC Qiqihar Rolling Stock for this rail car production initiative.

Gemco's Forrestfield facility in Perth will build the initial 40 rail cars, followed by 60 more at a new Karratha workshop starting later in 2025.

data.title: OCBC Prices EUR500 Million Worth of Bonds Due 2028
data.description:
Oversea-Chinese Banking Corp. or OCBC priced 500 million euros worth of 2.481% covered bonds due in 2028 under its $10 billion global covered bond program, according to a filing with the Singapore Exchange on Thursday.

The bonds will bear an interest rate of 2.481% per annum and are expected to be rated Aaa by Moody's Investors Services and AAA by Fitch Ratings.

Proceeds from the issue will be used for general corporate purposes.

Barclays Bank, Singapore Branch, ING Bank, Landesbank Baden-Wurttemberg, Oversea-Chinese Banking Corporation Limited, Societe Generale and UBS AG London Branch acted as the joint lead managers while Landesbank Hessen Thuringen Girozentrale acted as co-manager for this issue.

The bonds are expected to be issued on April 10.

data.title: Klarna tests the waters for a ‘normal’ IPO: podcast
data.description:
Follow on Apple or Spotify. Listen on the Reuters app. Read the episode transcript.

Listen to the podcast

The buy-now-pay-later firm is pushing through a still-thin pipeline toward a listing. Despite a high-tech sheen, it’s less tied to the big themes of the moment than recent disappointing offerings. In this week’s Viewsroom podcast, Breakingviews columnists debate its prospects.

Follow @Breakingviews on X
